title: The Moving Statue Game
description: Play music and let the child dance and move freely. When the music stops, the child must freeze in place like a statue. Keep playing while changing the tempo of the music.
goal: Improve impulse control, selective attention, and the ability to stop suddenly.
safety_notes: Ensure there is enough space to move safely. Watch the child to avoid bumping into objects.

title: Indoor Obstacle Course
description: Create a simple obstacle course using furniture and pillows. Ask the child to crawl under chairs, jump over pillows, walk on a straight line (tape), and so on. Give clear, sequential instructions.
goal: Enhance focus, motor planning, and following multi-step instructions.
safety_notes: Ensure all obstacles are safe and stable. Monitor the child to prevent falls or injuries.

title: Ball Toss with Rules
description: Sit with the child and toss the ball back and forth. Add a simple rule, like 'don't catch the ball if I say an animal's name' or 'only catch it if I say a color'. Change the rules periodically.
goal: Improve auditory attention, impulse control, and working memory.
safety_notes: Use a soft ball to avoid injuries. Ensure the space is clear of breakable objects.

title: Simple Yoga for Kids
description: Teach the child some simple yoga poses like tree pose, downward-facing dog, and mountain pose. Focus on deep breathing and maintaining balance in each pose.
goal: Improve body awareness, balance, focus, and energy regulation.
safety_notes: Ensure the child performs movements slowly and carefully. Do not force them into difficult poses. Stop if they feel any pain.

title: Guided Clapping Rhythms
description: Clap a specific pattern (e.g., clap-clap-pause-clap). Ask the child to imitate the same pattern. Gradually increase the complexity of the patterns or create longer rhythms.
goal: Enhance auditory attention, auditory memory, and motor coordination.
safety_notes: Ensure the child is sitting or standing in a comfortable position. Avoid excessive pressure if they struggle.

title: Collaborative Block Building Challenge
description: Take turns with the child to build a tower or a complex structure using blocks. Set a shared building goal (like 'we will build a tall castle'). Provide step-by-step instructions to the child during their turn.
goal: Develop joint attention, problem-solving skills, and following instructions within a collaborative activity.
safety_notes: Ensure the blocks are age-appropriate to avoid choking hazards. Maintain a fun and collaborative atmosphere.

title: Alternating Storytelling
description: Start a story with a sentence or two, then ask the child to add a sentence or two to continue the story. Keep taking turns. You can use picture cards as story prompts.
goal: Improve thought sequencing, sustained attention, and linguistic expression.
safety_notes: Let the child freely express their ideas without interruption. Keep the story fun and flexible.

title: Timed Household Chores
description: Choose a simple household chore (like tidying toys, putting dirty clothes in the basket). Set the timer for a short period (5-10 minutes) and encourage the child to finish the chore before the time runs out. Start with easy and short tasks.
goal: Develop time management skills, task focus, and a sense of accomplishment.
safety_notes: Ensure the tasks are safe and age-appropriate. Focus on the effort, not just the complete execution.

title: Memory Matching Game
materials_needed: Set of matching cards (pictures or words)
description: Spread the cards face down on the table. Take turns flipping two cards to find a matching pair. If you find a pair, keep it. If they don't match, flip them back over. The goal is to collect the most pairs.
goal: Improve sustained attention, visual memory, and focus.
safety_notes: Choose matching cards with clear and non-distracting images. Keep the game fun and engaging.

title: Guided Treasure Hunt
description: Hide a 'treasure' somewhere in the house or yard. Write or draw clues leading the child from one place to another until they reach the treasure. Read the first clue with the child and encourage them to think about the next step.
goal: Enhance focus, problem-solving skills, and following sequential instructions.
safety_notes: Choose safe hiding spots for clues and treasure. Ensure clues are clear and appropriate for the child's understanding level.

title: Focus and Distractions Quiz
description: A short quiz to help the child understand the importance of focus and how to handle distractions in their daily environment.
goal: Raise the child's awareness of distractions and how to maintain focus to improve attention.
safety_notes: Make the quiz fun and encouraging, not stressful. Provide support and positive reinforcement.

questions.question_text: What should you do if you're doing homework and hear the TV?
options: Turn off the TV or move to a quiet place.
Keep doing homework and watch TV.
questions.correct_answer: Turn off the TV or move to a quiet place.

questions.question_text: If your friend is talking to you and you're looking around, are you paying attention?
options: Yes, I'm listening with my eyes.
No, I should look at my friend to show I am listening.
It doesn't matter where I look.
questions.correct_answer: No, I should look at my friend to show I am listening.

questions.question_text: How can you remember long instructions adults give you?
options: Ask them to repeat it many times.
Think about something else while they talk.
Break it into small steps and try to repeat it in my mind.
questions.correct_answer: Break it into small steps and try to repeat it in my mind.

title: Impulse Control Challenge
description: A quiz posing scenarios requiring the child to think before acting to enhance impulse control skills.
goal: Develop the ability to consider consequences and make thoughtful decisions instead of acting impulsively.
safety_notes: Discuss answers with the child and provide constructive alternatives if they choose a non-ideal answer.

questions.question_text: If you see your friend's favorite toy and want to play with it right away, what do you do?
options: Ask nicely if I can play with it.
Wait until they leave it and then take it.
questions.correct_answer: Ask nicely if I can play with it.

questions.question_text: You are waiting your turn in a game, and suddenly feel bored and want to play now. What do you do?
options: Be patient and wait my turn, maybe find something else to do while I wait.
questions.correct_answer: Be patient and wait my turn, maybe find something else to do while I wait.

questions.question_text: The teacher asks a question and you know the answer. What is the best thing to do?
options: Raise my hand and wait to be called on.
Whisper the answer to my friend.
questions.correct_answer: Raise my hand and wait to be called on.

title: Daily Routine Helper
description: A quiz helping the child understand the importance of a daily routine and organizing tasks better.
goal: Encourage the child to organize their time and daily tasks to reduce distraction and forgetfulness.
safety_notes: Support the child in understanding the routine and how it helps. Keep it positive and motivating.

questions.question_text: Why is it important to have a morning routine?
options: So I can be fast and go to school late.
So I can organize my time and know what to do next.
Because there is nothing else to do.
questions.correct_answer: So I can organize my time and know what to do next.

questions.question_text: After returning from school, what is usually the first thing you should do?
options: Throw my bag and go play immediately.
Put my bag in its place and prepare for a snack or short rest.
Turn on the TV and watch my favorite show.
questions.correct_answer: Put my bag in its place and prepare for a snack or short rest.

questions.question_text: If you have several tasks to do (like homework, playing, cleaning the room), how do you decide what to do first?
options: Do the hardest first then the easiest.
Start with whatever comes to mind.
questions.correct_answer: Do the hardest first then the easiest.

title: Emotion Recognition Quiz
description: A quiz helping the child identify different emotions in themselves and others, and how to express them appropriately.
goal: Improve emotional awareness and the child's ability to regulate their emotional responses.
safety_notes: Encourage the child to express their feelings safely. There are no wrong answers regarding personal feelings, but there are better ways to handle them.

questions.question_text: If you feel angry, what is the best thing to do?
options: Take a deep breath and talk about my feelings calmly.
questions.correct_answer: Take a deep breath and talk about my feelings calmly.

questions.question_text: How is a person feeling when they are smiling and their eyes are bright?
questions.correct_answer: Happy.

questions.question_text: Your friend is crying because they fell. What feeling might you have?
options: Empathy and sadness for my friend.
questions.correct_answer: Empathy and sadness for my friend.

title: Body Movements and Attention Quiz
description: A quiz linking body movements to attention levels, helping the child understand how to use body movements to enhance focus.
goal: Help the child understand how organized movement can support focus and prevent excessive random movement.
safety_notes: Discuss with the child the importance of purposeful movements and their goal to improve attention.

questions.question_text: When you feel like moving a lot and can't focus in class, what can help you?
options: Jump out of my seat and run.
Ask permission to take a short break, do light movement, then return.
Try to sit perfectly still by force, even if it's hard.
questions.correct_answer: Ask permission to take a short break, do light movement, then return.

questions.question_text: If you feel sleepy during a lesson, what is a simple movement that can help you wake up a bit?
options: Tap my pen on the table.
Adjust my sitting position slightly or tense and relax my muscles.
Close my eyes and try to sleep.
questions.correct_answer: Adjust my sitting position slightly or tense and relax my muscles.

questions.question_text: Why might it be helpful to use a small stress ball while studying?
options: To help focus by providing mild sensory stimulation.
questions.correct_answer: To help focus by providing mild sensory stimulation.

title: Sensory Walk
materials_needed: Materials with different textures (carpet, soft fabric, rough fabric, sand, water in a bowl)
description: Create a short path using different materials. Ask the child to walk barefoot on each material and describe how it feels. This can be indoors or in a safe garden.
goal: Improve sensory integration, body awareness, and develop the ability to describe sensations.
safety_notes: Ensure all materials are clean, safe, and non-allergenic. Monitor the child closely to prevent falls or severe discomfort.

title: Deep Pressure Sandwich
description: Have the child lie down on a large blanket. Place another blanket and pillows gently on top of them (ensure they can breathe and get out easily). Press gently to 'make a sandwich'. Ask how they feel. Ensure the pressure is comforting and acceptable.
goal: Provide deep pressure sensory input, which can be calming and regulating for children with sensory sensitivities.
safety_notes: Do not leave the child unattended. Always ensure the pressure is comfortable and the child can breathe and move freely. Stop immediately if the child feels distressed.

title: Mirror Movement Imitation
materials_needed: A large mirror or sit face-to-face
description: Sit face-to-face with the child or in front of a mirror. Perform a simple movement (like raising an arm, shaking head, smiling), and ask the child to imitate it. Take turns; let the child make a movement and you imitate it.
goal: Improve imitation skills, joint attention, and understanding of non-verbal body language.
safety_notes: Start with simple, easy movements. Keep the game fun and unstressful. Celebrate their efforts regardless of accuracy.

title: Heavy Work Activities
materials_needed: Small wagon or basket full of toys
description: Ask the child to do activities involving resistance and pushing/pulling, such as: pushing a small wagon full of toys, pulling a basket, helping arrange heavy pillows, or using a hand pump to inflate a ball. These activities provide deep sensory input.
goal: Provide deep sensory input (proprioception) to help with self-regulation and reduce impulsive or repetitive behaviors.
safety_notes: Ensure activities are safe and do not cause excessive physical strain. Monitor the child to prevent injuries. Do not force the child into an activity they don't want to do.

title: Bubble Blowing Game
description: Blow bubbles and ask the child to chase them and try to touch or pop them. You can also encourage them to blow bubbles themselves to strengthen oral muscles.
goal: Improve visual-motor coordination, visual tracking, develop oral motor skills, and encourage joint attention.
safety_notes: Ensure the bubble solution is safe and non-toxic. Watch the child to prevent them from putting the solution in their mouth or eyes. Play in an open area.

title: Creating a Social Story
description: Choose a social situation the child finds difficult (like sharing toys, going to the doctor). Write a simple short story explaining the situation, what to expect, and how the child should appropriately behave. Use pictures to support the text.
goal: Teach social skills, understand social contexts, and reduce anxiety in new or challenging situations.
safety_notes: Make the story positive and supportive. Focus on desired behaviors and positive outcomes. Read the story with the child regularly.

title: Emotion Guessing Game
materials_needed: Picture cards of different facial expressions (happy, sad, angry)
description: Show picture cards of different facial expressions and ask the child to name the emotion. You or the child can also act out an emotion with face and body, and the other has to guess it.
goal: Develop emotion recognition and expression, and improve understanding of non-verbal expressions.
safety_notes: Start with basic, clear emotions. Don't pressure the child if they struggle. Provide help and guidance.

title: Turn-Taking Games
materials_needed: Simple board game (like Snakes and Ladders)
description: Play a game with the child that clearly requires turn-taking, like a board game or building with blocks where you each take a turn. Emphasize phrases like 'My turn now' and 'Your turn now'.
goal: Teach turn-taking skills, sharing, and patience in social interactions.
safety_notes: Choose simple, age-appropriate games. Keep it fun and offer positive reinforcement for the child following turn-taking rules.

title: Joint Attention Activities
description: Sit with the child and direct their attention to the same object. For example, point to a picture in a book and say 'Look at the dog!', then wait for the child's reaction. Or play with a toy together and share comments ('We are building a tall tower!').
goal: Develop joint attention skills, which is the cornerstone of social interactions and communication.
safety_notes: Start with very short activities. Do not pressure the child. Repeat pointing and commenting patiently. Celebrate any sign of joint attention.

title: Role-Playing and Pretend Play
description: Engage the child in role-playing and pretend play. For example, pretend you are a doctor and a patient, a teacher and a student, or a shopkeeper and a customer. Encourage the child to imitate actions and expressions related to the role.
goal: Enhance imaginative play skills, understanding of social roles, and develop flexibility in thinking and interacting.
safety_notes: Start with simple, familiar scenarios. Allow the child to choose the role or scenario first if they have a preference. Be a good role model.

title: Facial Emotion Recognition Quiz
description: A quiz aiming to teach the child to recognize basic emotions through facial expressions in photos or drawings.
goal: Improve the child's ability to decode non-verbal social cues, especially facial expressions.
safety_notes: Provide clear, non-distracting images. Encourage the child to notice details in the face (eyes, mouth).

questions.question_text: Someone is smiling and their mouth is slightly open, how are they feeling?
questions.correct_answer: Happy

questions.question_text: Someone is frowning and their eyebrows are drawn together, how are they likely feeling?
questions.correct_answer: Angry

questions.question_text: Someone's eyes are wide open and their mouth is slightly open like a gasp, how are they feeling?
questions.correct_answer: Surprised

title: Social Skills Scenarios
description: A quiz presenting social scenarios and asking the child to choose the appropriate response, enhancing their understanding of acceptable social behaviors.
goal: Develop social decision-making skills, understanding social expectations, and promoting positive interactions.
safety_notes: Discuss each scenario and answer in detail with the child. Provide logical reasoning for correct answers and help them understand their mistakes.

questions.question_text: If your friend wants to share their favorite toy with you, what do you do?
options: Take it from them without saying anything.
Thank them and play with them.
Refuse to share and play alone.
questions.correct_answer: Thank them and play with them.

questions.question_text: You see your friend crying, what do you do?
options: Ask them what's wrong and if they need help.
questions.correct_answer: Ask them what's wrong and if they need help.

questions.question_text: The teacher is talking in class, and you want to ask a question. What is the right thing to do?
options: Interrupt the teacher and ask my question.
Raise my hand and wait my turn.
Shout the question from my seat.
questions.correct_answer: Raise my hand and wait my turn.

title: Sensory Preferences Quiz
description: A quiz helping the child identify their sensory preferences and avoidances, aiding them in expressing their sensory needs better.
goal: Increase self-awareness of the child's sensory needs and provide strategies for sensory regulation.
safety_notes: There are no 'right' or 'wrong' answers in the traditional sense. The goal is to understand preferences. Discuss why they prefer some things over others.

questions.question_text: When you hear a loud and sudden noise, what do you prefer to do?
options: Cover my ears or go to a quieter place.
questions.correct_answer: Cover my ears or go to a quieter place.

questions.question_text: What kind of clothing do you usually prefer?
questions.correct_answer: Soft and comfortable clothes.

questions.question_text: Do you prefer foods with strong flavors and lots of spices, or mild-tasting foods?
questions.correct_answer: Mild taste.

title: Communication Choices Quiz
description: A quiz presenting scenarios that require the child to choose the most appropriate method of communication (verbal, pointing, using pictures).
goal: Enhance functional communication skills, enabling the child to express their needs and desires effectively.
safety_notes: Focus on the importance of clear communication. Encourage the child to use all available communication tools.

questions.question_text: You want to ask your mother for a glass of water, what is the best way to ask?
options: Point to the water and shout.
Say 'Water please' or point to a picture of water if using communication cards.
Wait until my mother asks me what I want.
questions.correct_answer: Say 'Water please' or point to a picture of water if using communication cards.

questions.question_text: If you don't understand what someone is saying, what do you do?
options: Ask them to repeat themselves or explain in a different way.
questions.correct_answer: Ask them to repeat themselves or explain in a different way.

questions.question_text: What is the best way to ask for help when you are in trouble?
options: Go to an adult and say 'I need help'.
questions.correct_answer: Go to an adult and say 'I need help'.

title: Friendship Skills Quiz
description: A quiz exploring scenarios about building and maintaining friendships, such as sharing, cooperation, and understanding friends' feelings.
goal: Enhance the child's understanding of friendship concepts, sharing, and empathy necessary for effective social interaction.
safety_notes: Discuss the importance of being a good friend. Emphasize that friendships require effort and mutual consideration.

questions.question_text: Your friend wants to play a game you don't really like. What could you do?
options: Play their game for a while, then ask if we can play my game next.
Force them to play my game instead.
questions.correct_answer: Play their game for a while, then ask if we can play my game next.

questions.question_text: You and your friend both want the same piece of cake, but there's only one left. What's a fair solution?
options: We cut it in half so we both get a piece.
I cry until they give it to me.
questions.correct_answer: We cut it in half so we both get a piece.

questions.question_text: Your friend tells you a secret. What should you do?
options: Keep the secret to show I am a trustworthy friend.
questions.correct_answer: Keep the secret to show I am a trustworthy friend.

title: Letter Tracing in Sand
description: Fill a tray with a thin layer of sand or salt. Ask the child to trace letters with their finger. Say the letter name and its sound while they trace it. Erase by shaking the tray and repeat with different letters.
goal: Strengthen letter recognition and formation through tactile multi-sensory learning.
safety_notes: Ensure the child does not put sand or salt in their mouth or eyes. Use a clean, food-safe material if possible.

title: Clay Letter Building
description: Give the child modeling clay and ask them to form specific letters. They can look at a reference card while shaping. Once done, ask them to say the letter name and a word that starts with it.
goal: Improve letter recognition, fine motor skills, and phonemic awareness through hands-on manipulation.
safety_notes: Use non-toxic clay. Supervise younger children to prevent them from putting clay in their mouth.

title: Syllable Jumping
materials_needed: Chalk or tape for floor markers
description: Draw circles or place tape markers on the floor. Say a word and ask the child to jump from one marker to the next for each syllable (e.g., 'wa-ter-mel-on' = 4 jumps). Clap along to reinforce the rhythm.
goal: Develop phonological awareness and the ability to segment words into syllables.
safety_notes: Ensure the floor surface is not slippery. Place markers far enough apart that the child can jump safely.

title: Air Writing
description: Ask the child to stand up and 'write' letters in the air using their whole arm. Make it big and exaggerated. Say the letter sound as they write. You can also have them write short words one letter at a time.
goal: Reinforce letter formation and directionality using gross motor movement and muscle memory.
safety_notes: Ensure there is enough space around the child so they do not hit anything while moving their arms.

title: Sound Sorting Relay
materials_needed: Two baskets or boxes labeled with starting sounds
description: Place two baskets on one side of the room, each labeled with a different starting sound (e.g., 'B' and 'S'). Scatter picture cards on the other side. The child runs to pick up a card, says the object name, identifies its starting sound, and places it in the correct basket.
goal: Improve phonemic awareness, initial sound identification, and physical engagement with reading concepts.
safety_notes: Clear the running path of any obstacles. Use picture cards with clear, unambiguous images.

title: Read-Along with Finger Tracking
description: Read a story together. The parent reads aloud while the child follows along by pointing to each word with their finger. Pause occasionally and ask the child to read a simple word they recognize. Celebrate their attempts.
goal: Build word tracking skills, print awareness, and reading confidence in a supportive environment.
safety_notes: Choose books at or slightly below the child's reading level to avoid frustration. Keep sessions short and positive.

title: Rhyme Time Game
description: Take turns saying words that rhyme. Start with a simple word like 'cat' and go back and forth: 'hat', 'bat', 'mat'. If the child gets stuck, give them a hint or a silly made-up word to keep it fun. Try different starting words each round.
goal: Strengthen phonological awareness and the ability to recognize and produce rhyming patterns.
safety_notes: Keep the game lighthearted. Accept made-up words that rhyme to encourage participation and creativity.

title: Word Building with Letter Tiles
materials_needed: A flat surface or magnetic board
description: Give the child a set of letter tiles. Say a simple word (e.g., 'dog') and ask them to build it. Then change one letter to make a new word ('log', 'dig'). Work through word families together, discussing the sounds as you swap letters.
goal: Develop decoding skills, spelling awareness, and understanding of how changing one letter changes the whole word.
safety_notes: Ensure letter tiles are large enough to not be a choking hazard for younger children.

title: Audiobook and Follow-Along
materials_needed: Physical copy of the same book
description: Play an audiobook while the child follows along in the physical book. Pause the audio occasionally and ask the child to continue reading the next sentence on their own. Discuss the story together afterward.
goal: Connect spoken and written language, improve reading fluency, and build reading stamina without frustration.
safety_notes: Choose audiobooks narrated at a moderate pace. Do not force the child to read long passages independently if they are struggling.

title: Spelling Dictation Practice
description: Say a word clearly and use it in a sentence. Ask the child to write the word. If they misspell it, show them the correct spelling, have them trace it with their finger, say each letter aloud, and try again. Focus on 5-8 words per session.
goal: Strengthen spelling accuracy, auditory processing, and the connection between sounds and written letters.
safety_notes: Keep the word list short to prevent fatigue. Always end on a word the child gets right to maintain confidence.

title: Letter Recognition Quiz
description: A quiz helping the child practice distinguishing between commonly confused letters and understanding letter orientation.
goal: Improve visual discrimination of letters and reduce letter reversal errors.
safety_notes: Present questions in a fun, game-like manner. Celebrate correct answers and gently guide through mistakes.

questions.question_text: Which of these letters looks different from the others: b, b, d, b?
questions.correct_answer: The third one (d)

questions.question_text: If you see the word 'was' in a mirror, what might it look like?
questions.correct_answer: saw

questions.question_text: Which letter is 'p' flipped upside down?
questions.correct_answer: d

title: Rhyming Words Quiz
description: A quiz testing the child's ability to identify words that rhyme, strengthening their phonological awareness.
goal: Improve the child's ability to hear and recognize rhyming patterns, a foundational skill for reading.
safety_notes: Keep the tone playful. If the child struggles, say the words aloud and exaggerate the rhyming sounds.

questions.question_text: Which word rhymes with 'cat'?
questions.correct_answer: Hat

questions.question_text: Which word does NOT rhyme with 'dog'?
questions.correct_answer: Dig

questions.question_text: Pick the pair of words that rhyme:
questions.correct_answer: Tree and Free

title: Syllable Counting Quiz
description: A quiz where the child counts the number of syllables in spoken words, reinforcing their ability to break words into smaller parts.
goal: Develop syllable segmentation skills, which are critical for decoding longer words during reading.
safety_notes: Say each word slowly and clearly. Encourage the child to clap along to count syllables if they find it helpful.

questions.question_text: How many syllables are in the word 'banana'?
How many syllables are in the word 'cat'?
Which word has more syllables: 'elephant' or 'dog'?
questions.correct_answer: Elephant

title: Reading Strategies Quiz
description: A quiz that teaches the child practical strategies for when they encounter a difficult word while reading.
goal: Equip the child with self-help strategies to decode unfamiliar words independently.
safety_notes: Discuss each strategy after the quiz. Practice applying them together with a real book passage.

questions.question_text: You see a long word you don't know while reading. What is a good first step?
options: Try to break the word into smaller parts or syllables.
questions.correct_answer: Try to break the word into smaller parts or syllables.

questions.question_text: If you don't understand a sentence you just read, what should you do?
options: Keep reading and hope it makes sense later.
Read the sentence again slowly and look at the picture for clues.
questions.correct_answer: Read the sentence again slowly and look at the picture for clues.

questions.question_text: What can help you remember what you read?
options: Stopping after each page to think about what happened.
questions.correct_answer: Stopping after each page to think about what happened.

title: Spelling Patterns Quiz
description: A quiz helping the child recognize common spelling patterns and word families, making it easier to read and spell new words.
goal: Build awareness of common letter patterns so the child can generalize spelling rules to unfamiliar words.
safety_notes: Focus on patterns the child has already been exposed to. Introduce new patterns gradually.

questions.question_text: The words 'light', 'night', and 'right' all share the same ending. What is it?
questions.correct_answer: -ight

questions.question_text: If 'cake' has a silent 'e' that makes the 'a' say its name, which of these also follows that rule?
questions.correct_answer: Make

questions.question_text: Which word is spelled correctly?
questions.correct_answer: Friend
